summary: Japanese architect (b. 1941, Osaka), self-taught, won the Pritzker Prize in 1995. Signature style: exposed reinforced concrete as a medium for controlling natural light. Best known for the Church of the Light in Ibaraki, Osaka (cross-shaped slit in east concrete wall). Other key works: Chapel on the Water (Hokkaido; glass-and-steel cube intersecting concrete prism; floor-to-ceiling window aligned with reflecting pool bearing a cross), Azuma House / Row House (1976, Osaka; completely blank concrete facade, no exterior windows), Chichu Art Museum (Naoshima; subterranean; geometric skylights). Won the Pritzker Prize in 1995.

works.name: Church of the Light
works.description: Protestant church in Ibaraki, Osaka (1989). The only light source is a cruciform (cross-shaped) slit cut into the east concrete wall, creating a dramatic light cross over the altar. The walls are cast-concrete slabs cut at a 15-degree angle. Benches and floors are made from repurposed scaffolding planks. The building has no doors or windows (only the cross-shaped gap). Also described as having an open-air passageway entrance leading into the blinding-light interior. Often paired with or contrasted to the earlier Chapel on the Water.

clues.clue: Cross-shaped / cruciform slit in the east concrete wall is the only light source; benches and floor from repurposed scaffolding planks
examples: name this Japanese architect who designed Osaka's Church of the Light
This modern Japanese architect allowed light to stream in through a cross cut in the concrete wall of his Church of the Light
receives natural light from a cross-shaped slit on the back wall
This architect designed 'The Church of the Light' in Ibaraki, which uses scaffolding planks for pews
Two slabs of silky smooth concrete that are cut at a 15 degree angle comprise an Osaka chapel

clues.clue: No doors or windows; open-air passageway entrance leads to interior blinded by light flooding through two intersecting gaps
examples: That one of these buildings, which has no doors or windows, was designed by Tadao Ando
the entrance to that one of these buildings is an open air passageway that leads into a room where onlookers are blinded by light flooding in through two intersecting gaps in the far wall

works.name: Chapel on the Water
works.description: Chapel (1988) attached to the Alpha Resort Hotel on Hokkaido. Employs 'borrowed scenery': a floor-to-ceiling glass-and-steel cube intersects with a concrete prism; a floor-to-ceiling window is level with a reflecting pool with a cross at the center. Cited by architecture critic Kenneth Frampton as an example of 'Critical Regionalism.' Sometimes called 'Church on the Water.'

clues.clue: Chapel on the Water / Church on the Water: glass-and-steel cube intersecting concrete prism; floor-to-ceiling window aligned with reflecting pool bearing a cross; 'borrowed scenery'; Hokkaido
examples: In a design by this architect that employs 'borrowed scenery,' a floor-to-ceiling window is level with a reflecting pool with a cross in the center

Frampton singles out the work of Tadao Ando, who designed this building attached to the Alpha Resort Hotel on Hokkaido. This religious building consists of a glass-and-steel cube intersecting with a concrete prism
Four crosses made of this material appear in a complex whose floor-to-ceiling window opens to a reflecting pool

works.name: Azuma House / Row House
works.description: 1976 row house in Osaka (also called the Azuma House or Sumiyoshi Row House). A completely blank prefabricated concrete facade with no exterior windows. Often cited alongside the Church of the Light as the two defining early works of Ando's signature concrete-and-light approach. The interior is organized around a narrow courtyard open to the sky.

clues.clue: 1976 row house / Azuma House: completely blank concrete facade, no exterior windows
examples: A 1976 row house by this architect has no exterior windows on its facade
a townhouse with a completely blank prefabricated concrete facade
This is the main material used by an architect for the Rokko Housing series and a windowless 'Row House'

works.name: Chichu Art Museum
works.description: Subterranean art museum on Naoshima island, Japan (2004). Built almost entirely underground; natural light enters through concrete-framed geometric skylights (triangular, square, and cylindrical openings). Houses works by Claude Monet, James Turrell, and Walter De Maria. Continues Ando's signature theme of controlling light through concrete geometry.

clues.clue: Chichu Art Museum: subterranean / underground; lets in light through concrete-reinforced geometric openings
examples: This architect's subterranean Chichu Art Museum lets in light through concrete-reinforced geometric openings, a technique he also used in his Church of the Light
His subterranean Chichu Art Museum lets in light through concrete-reinforced geometric openings

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Tadao Ando (b. 1941, Osaka) is a self-taught Japanese architect known for a Brutalist-inflected style centered on exposed reinforced concrete and the manipulation of natural light. Won the 1995 Pritzker Prize. Other notable works: Hill of the Buddha (Hokkaido; artificial hill planted with lavender surrounding a colossal Buddha statue), 21_21 Design Sight Tokyo (trapezoidal hand-sanded steel roof), Wood Culture Museum in Kansai (truncated conical form), Benesse Art House Project on Naoshima (contains James Turrell's Back Side of the Moon; yakisugi/charred wood used), Bourse de Commerce Paris (cylindrical concrete wall for Francois Pinault), Rokko Housing (Hyogo; built on 60-degree slope), 4×4 House (post-earthquake client; replicated in wood on neighboring plot), maritime museum on Saadiyat Island Abu Dhabi, Punta della Dogana in Venice (triangular art museum between two canals; restored). Self-taught architect who learned by reading books and traveling. Inspired by Frank Lloyd Wright's Imperial Hotel Tokyo.

clues.clue: Self-taught Japanese architect; won the Pritzker Prize in 1995; signature: exposed reinforced concrete + manipulation of natural light
examples: this self-taught architect who won the 1995 Pritzker Prize

comprehensive_summary:
Tadao Ando (b. 1941, Osaka) is a self-taught Japanese architect who won the Pritzker Prize in 1995. His signature style centers on bare exposed reinforced concrete as a medium for orchestrating natural light.

The Church of the Light (1989) in Ibaraki, Osaka is Ando's defining work. It is a Protestant church whose only light source is a cruciform slit cut into the east concrete wall, projecting a cross of light onto the altar. The walls are concrete slabs angled at 15 degrees; pews and floors are made from repurposed scaffolding planks. The building has no conventional doors or windows — only the cross-shaped gap. An open-air passageway leads into the blinding interior. Cited constantly as the giveaway clue for Ando.

The Chapel on the Water (1988) on Hokkaido, attached to the Alpha Resort Hotel, employs 'borrowed scenery': a glass-and-steel cube intersects a concrete prism, and a floor-to-ceiling window aligns with a reflecting pool containing a cross at its center. Architecture critic Kenneth Frampton cited it as an example of Critical Regionalism.

The Azuma House / Row House (1976) in Osaka is an early benchmark work: a 1976 row house with a completely blank prefabricated concrete facade and no exterior windows. Often paired with the Church of the Light as the two canonical early Ando works.

The Chichu Art Museum (2004) on Naoshima island is almost entirely subterranean, with natural light entering through concrete-framed geometric skylights. It houses works by Monet, James Turrell, and Walter De Maria.

Other notable works: Hill of the Buddha (Hokkaido; lavender-covered artificial hill surrounding a colossal Buddha), Benesse Art House Project on Naoshima (contains James Turrell's Back Side of the Moon; yakisugi/charred wood exterior), 21_21 Design Sight in Tokyo (trapezoidal hand-sanded steel roof), Wood Culture Museum in Kansai (truncated conical form), Rokko Housing (Hyogo; built on 60-degree slope), Punta della Dogana in Venice (triangular art museum restored by Ando, between two canals, topped by the Fortune sculpture), Bourse de Commerce Paris (cylindrical concrete wall for Francois Pinault), and mansions for Beyonce (Malibu, $200 million) and Kanye West (gutted). As a self-taught architect, Ando was inspired by Frank Lloyd Wright's Imperial Hotel in Tokyo.

Building: 'borrowed scenery'; glass-and-steel cube intersecting concrete prism; floor-to-ceiling window level with a reflecting pool bearing a cross; Hokkaido; cited by Kenneth Frampton for Critical Regionalism
Building: 1976 row house (Azuma House); completely blank concrete facade; no exterior windows; Osaka
Building: subterranean museum on Naoshima; natural light through concrete-framed geometric skylights; houses Monet, Turrell, De Maria works
Architect: self-taught Japanese architect; won 1995 Pritzker Prize; signature style: exposed reinforced concrete + manipulation of natural light
Architect: Hill of the Buddha — artificial hill planted with lavender surrounding a colossal Buddha statue (Hokkaido)
Architect: restored Punta della Dogana, Venice — triangular museum between two canals topped by sculpture of two slaves holding a golden ball with Fortune on a wind vane
